---
title: Networked Narratives
subtitle: Leveraging LLMs and GNNs for Next-Generation Knowledge Graphs
format: 
    clean-revealjs:
        self-contained: true
        footer: "Filip Wójcik | NeuraSYS | Wroclaw University of Economics"
        background-size: contain
        background-position: center
        background-repeat: no-repeat
        transition: fade

author:
  - name: Filip Wójcik, Ph.D.
    roles: Senior Data Scientist, Researcher
    url: https://filip-wojcik.com
    email: ds@filip-wojcik.com
    orcid: 0000-0001-5938-7260
    affiliation:
      - name: Wroclaw University of Economics
        url: https://ue.wroc.pl/en/
      - name: Piwik PRO
        url: https://filip-wojcik.com
date: last-modified
---

# Agenda

1. Challenges in structural reasoning with LLMs.
2. Fundamentals of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs).
3. Techniques for extracting knowledge graphs from text using LLMs.
4. Integrating LLM-extracted graphs with GNNs.
5. Case study: Wikipedia article analysis with HeXtractor.

# Introduction

## The Challenge of Structural Reasoning

Large Language Models excel at text generation but struggle with structural relationships between entities.

This limitation manifests as:

- **Hallucinations** when processing complex knowledge domains;
- **Missed analytical insights** in interconnected data;
- **Inability to leverage graph topology** for reasoning.

## Graph Neural Networks: A Silent Revolution

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
Since 2017, Graph Neural Networks have undergone substantial architectural advances:

- **Message-passing** (MPNN) framework formalization;
- **Knowledge graph integration** methods were introduced;
- **Heterogeneous graph processing** capabilities were significantly expanded.
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](./figures/concept_graph.png){width=90%}
:::
:::

**Despite this progress, GNNs remain underutilized in mainstream AI systems compared to LLMs.**

## Ongoing research

::: {#fig-text2graph layout-ncol=2 }

![KG and LLMs in action](./figures/manning.png){#fig-manning}

![Neo4j Graph DS](./figures/neo4j.gif){#fig-neo4j}

Recent publications and libraries utilizing Knowledge Graphs and LLMs for structured reasoning.
:::

# GNN Fundamentals

## Graph Neural Networks Overview

::: {.larger}
GNNs process structured data by learning representations of nodes and edges through iterative message passing.

**Input requirements:**

- Node attribute matrices for each node type, $\mathbf{X}_v \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d_v}$
- Edge attribute matrices for relationship types, $\mathbf{E}_{v,u} \in \mathbb{R}^{|E| \times d_e}$
- Adjacency structures defining connectivity, typically as **adjacency lists** or **edge index tensors**, $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{N}^{2 \times |E|}$

The architecture transforms discrete graph structures into continuous embeddings suitable for downstream tasks.
:::

![](./figures/gnn_overview.png){fig-align="center"}

::: {.smaller}
Jure Leskovec, Stanford CS224W: Machine Learning with Graphs, http://cs224w.stanford.edu
:::


## Message-Passing Architecture {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Iterative Process (K steps):**

1. **Neighborhood Collection**: Identify direct neighbors $\mathcal{N}(v)$ for each vertex $v$
2. **Message Construction**: Generate messages from neighbors using learned mappings
3. **Aggregation**: Combine neighbor messages (mean, sum, attention mechanisms)
4. **Node Update**: Integrate aggregated information with current node representation
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](./figures/mpnn_architecture.png)
:::
:::

Each iteration enables nodes to incorporate information from progressively distant neighborhoods, propagating structural patterns throughout the network.

## Message Passing Visualization

![](./figures/mpnn_architecture.png)


## Heterogeneous Graphs

**Heterogeneous graphs** represent a specialized case where nodes and edges possess distinct types and properties.

**Formal Definition:**

$$G = (V, E, A, R, \phi, \psi)$$


::: {.small}
Where

* $V$ is the set of vertices (nodes);
* $E$ is the set of edges;
* $A$ is the set of node types;
* $R$ is the set of relationship types;
* $\phi: V \rightarrow A$ maps vertices to types;
* $\psi: E \rightarrow R$ maps edges to relationship types.
:::
This heterogeneity requires specialized GNN architectures that handle varying attribute dimensions across node and edge types.

## Heterogeneous Graphs Visualization

![](./figures/hetero_graph_visualization.png)

::: {.small}
[Teaching old labels new tricks in heterogeneous graphs, Google Research](https://research.google/blog/teaching-old-labels-new-tricks-in-heterogeneous-graphs/) 
:::

## Knowledge Graphs

:::{.callout-tip}
## Knowledge graphs vs heterogeneous graphs

- **Knowledge graph**: 
  - Curated semantic entities and relations (often an informal term).
  - Often represented as ontology or RDF/property graph.
- **Heterogeneous graph**: 
    - GNN term for graphs with multiple node/edge types.
    - Tensor (numbers) based.
- **Similarity**: both feature various types of nodes and edges.
- The terms are sometimes used interchangeably in literature.
:::

![](./figures/neo4jkg.png){width=50% fig-align="center"}

::: {.small .text-center}
[Neo4j: What Is a Knowledge Graph?](https://neo4j.com/blog/knowledge-graph/what-is-knowledge-graph/)
:::

## GNN Tasks

**Primary Applications:**

![](./figures/gnn_tasks.png){fig-align="center"}

Each task leverages learned embeddings that encode both node attributes and topological position within the network.

# Changing text to graph

## The Compatibility Gap

Standard GNN architectures require numerical inputs, while knowledge exists predominantly in unstructured text.

**Traditional NLP Approaches:**

- Dependency parsing generates syntactic graphs
- Libraries like SpaCy and NLTK enable sentence-level decomposition
- Focus typically limited to grammatical relationships

![](./figures/displacy.png){width=60%}

## Syntactic Tree Parsing Visualization

![](./figures/syntax_tree.png)

::: {.small}
[Syntax Tree - Natural Language Processing, Geeks for geeks](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/machine-learning/syntax-tree-natural-language-processing/)
:::

## Knowledge Graph Requirements

Modern analysis demands high-level semantic connections spanning multiple sentences and documents.

**Key Differences from Syntactic Parsing:**

- **Entity relationships** rather than grammatical dependencies
- **Cross-document connections** and conceptual hierarchies
- **Domain-specific relationship types**
- **Handling of implicit and indirect connections**

This semantic extraction challenge represents the bridge between unstructured text and GNN-compatible structures.

# LLMs for Knowledge Graph Extraction

## LLM Capabilities

::: {columns}

::: {.column width="80%"}
Recent research demonstrates that LLMs with extended context windows can extract semantic graph elements through structured prompting.

**Extraction Process:**

1. 🛢️ Define target schema (node types, relationship types)
2. 👤 Instruct LLM to identify entities and connections
3. { } Enforce structured output formatting
4. ✅ Validate consistency across extracted elements

The approach leverages LLMs' understanding of domain knowledge and relationship semantics.
:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
![](./figures/langchain-color.png){width=90%}
![](./figures/crew-ai.png){width=90%}
:::
:::
## Implementation Approaches

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.larger}
**Custom Prompting:**

```python
class Node(BaseModel):
    id: str
    node_type: str

class Edge(BaseModel):
    source_node_id: str
    target_node_id: str
    edge_type: str

class Graph(BaseModel):
    nodes: list[Node]
    edges: list[Edge]

llm_with_structured_output = llm.with_structured_output(Graph)
system_prompt = SystemPrompt("""
You are a helpful assistant for extracting structured knowledge graphs from text. 
Your task is to read the provided text and convert it into a graph structure.
Follow the schema below:
        For each input, identify and extract:
        - Concepts, disciplines, and the relationships between them
        - People, organizations, and places mentioned in the text
        - Associations and interactions among these entities
""")
...
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**LangChain Components:**

- `LLMGraphTransformer` provides structured extraction
- `GraphDocument` encapsulates results
- Experimental features under active development
:::
:::

**Both approaches transform raw text into semantic triples representing domain knowledge.**

## LLM Extraction Outputs

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Example nodes

```python
Node(
    id='Machine learning', 
    node_type='Concept')

Node(
    id='AI', 
    node_type='Concept')

Node(
    id='Deep learning', 
    node_type='Concept')
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Example edges
```python
# Example edges
Edge(source=Node(
        id='Machine learning', 
        type='Concept'), 
    target=Node(
        id='Artificial intelligence',
         type='Concept'),
    type='is_subfield_of')

Edge(source=Node(
        id='Deep learning', 
        type='Concept'), 
    target=Node(
        id='Machine learning',
         type='Concept'),
    type='is_subfield_of')
```
:::
:::

## LLM Extraction Outputs Visualization

![](./figures/concept_graph.png){fig-align="center"}


## Limitations and Considerations

**Extraction Challenges:**

- 🎲 Model stochasticity affects reproducibility;
- 🏅 Quality varies with model capability and size;
- 💬 Requires careful prompt engineering for domain specificity;
- 🧩 Entity normalization and disambiguation needed;
- 🔄 Relationship consistency is not guaranteed.

:::{.callout-tip}
## Model selection
Standard LLM tuning and selection techniques can help (e.g., temperature ≈ 0 for deterministic outputs, structured output enforcement, etc.).
:::


# LLM-GNN Integration Architecture

## Processing Pipeline

::: {#fig-pipeline layout-ncol=2 }
![Pipeline](./figures/pipeline.svg){#fig-pipeline-concept height=500px}

![Pipeline visualization](./figures/hextractor_flow_img.png){#fig-hextractor-flow}

From text to knowledge graph; from knowledge graph to graph neural network.

:::

# HeXtractor: Automation Framework

## HeXtractor Library

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="70%"}

::: {.larger}

HeXtractor (created together with **Marcin Maleczeski** @ Piwik PRO) automates heterogeneous graph construction from both textual and tabular sources.

**Key Features:**

- Converts text to `LangChain GraphDocuments`
- Transforms `LangChain GraphDocuments` to graph neural network format
- Handles node ID mapping and validation
- Supports multiple encoding strategies
- Provides visualization capabilities
:::
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

![](./figures/pyg_logo.png){height=20%}
![](./figures/langchain-color.png){height=20%}

:::

::::


::: {.small}
[Wójcik et al., (2025). HeXtractor: Extracting Heterogeneous Graphs from Structured and Textual Data for Graph Neural Networks. Journal of Open Source Software, 10(110), 8057](https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.08057)

{{< fa brands github >}} Github: [https://github.com/maddataanalyst/hextractor](https://github.com/maddataanalyst/hextractor)
:::

::: {.smaller}
1. Wójcik, F. (2025). Money Laundering Detection with Multi-Aggregation Custom Edge GIN. Journal of Data Science, 1-19.
2. Wójcik, F. (2024). An analysis of novel money laundering data using heterogeneous graph isomorphism networks. FinCEN files case study. Econometrics. Ekonometria. Advances in Applied Data Analytics, 28(2), 32-49.
:::

## HeXtractor overview

![](./figures/hextractor_use_cases.png)

# HeXtractor overview - code

```python

# Step 1: Given a text...
text = """Filip Wójcik and Marcin Malczewski are data scientists, 
who developed HeXtractor. It is a library that helps 
in extracting heterogeneous knowledge graphs 
from various data sources. Heterogeneous knowledge graphs are graphs, 
that contain different types of nodes and edges."""

docs = [Document(page_content=text)]

# Step 2: Use LLM to extract knowledge graph
llm = ChatOpenAI(base_url=base_url, api_key=api_key)
llm_graph_transformer = LLMGraphTransformer(llm=llm)

kknowledge_graph = await llm_graph_transformer.aconvert_to_graph_documents(docs)  

# Step 3: Convert to graph neural network format
hetero_data, node_mapping = hextractor.convert_graph_document_to_hetero_data(knowledge_graph)

# Step 3: Visualize, train GNN, etc.
visualization = viz.VisualizationBuilder.build_visualization(
  simple_viz_cfg, 
  hetero_data, 
  mapped_node_names)

gnn = GNN(hetero_data)
...
```

# Case Study: Wikipedia Analysis

## Experimental Setup

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}

::: {.larger}
**Objective:** Extract conceptual relationships from machine learning literature and generate structure-aware embeddings.

**Data Sources:**

Wikipedia articles on:

1. Machine Learning
2. Neural Networks
3. Deep Learning

**Target Tasks:** 

1. Link prediction for `is_subfield_of` relationships between ML concepts
2. Embedding visualization for concepts and researchers.

**Model Configuration:** GPT-4.1-mini.
:::
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](./figures/wikipedia_logo.jpg){width=90%}
:::
:::

## 1. Text ➡️ 2. Knowledge Graph via LLM

::: {#fig-text2graph layout-ncol=2 }

![Wikipedia article](./figures/wikipedia_txt.png){#fig-wikipedia-txt height=50%}

![Semantic graph](./figures/hextractor_graph.png){#fig-semantic-graph}

::: {.small}
Wikipedia article transformed into knowledge graph via LLM.
:::
:::


## 2. Knowledge graph ➡️ 3. Heterogeneous Graph

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

::: {.larger}
**HeteroData Structure**

1. Node types: concept, person, organization
2. Each node of type *t* has numerical attribute matrix $\mathbf{X}_{v}^{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{|V_t| \times d_t}$
3. Each relationship type $(t_u, r, t_v)$ has edge index tensor $\mathbf{E}_{t_u, r, t_v} \in \mathbb{N}^{2 \times |E_{t_u, r, t_v}|}$
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

```python
HeteroData(
  concept={ x=[40, 16] },
  person={ x=[10, 8] },
  organization={ x=[8, 4] },
  (concept, is_subfield_of, concept)={ 
    edge_index=[2, 16]
},
  (concept, is_model_used_in, concept)={ 
    edge_index=[2, 10] 
},
  (person, contributed_to, concept)={ 
    edge_index=[2, 6] 
},
  ...
)
```
:::
:::

The extraction identified 40 distinct concepts with 16 hierarchical relationships suitable for GNN training.


##  3. Heterogeneous Graph ➡️ 4. GNN (link pred.)

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.larger}
**Model Configuration:**

- GraphSAGE (2 layers, 16 hidden dim);
- Link prediction with negative sampling;
- Graph split for train/validation/test.

**Training Setup**

- Supervised learning: predicting positive edges
- Negative sampling for nonexistent edges
- K-step neighborhood sampling for each node

:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./figures/training_results.png)
:::
:::

The network successfully distinguished true connections from negative samples, demonstrating effective learning of structural patterns.

## GNN structure

![](./figures/SimpleCNN.svg)

## GNN code


::: {.larger}
```python
GraphModule(
  (convs): ModuleList(
    (0): ModuleDict(
      (Concept__is_subfield_of__Concept): SAGEConv((-1, -1), 16, aggr=['sum', 'mean'])
      (Person__coined_term_for__Concept): SAGEConv((-1, -1), 16, aggr=['sum', 'mean'])
      ...
      (Organization__used_for_application__Concept): SAGEConv((-1, -1), 16, aggr=['sum', 'mean'])
      (Concept__rev_coined_term_for__Person): SAGEConv((-1, -1), 16, aggr=['sum', 'mean'])
      (Concept__rev_used_for_application__Organization): SAGEConv((-1, -1), 16, aggr=['sum', 'mean'])
    )
  )
  (act): ModuleDict(
    (Concept): ReLU()
    (Person): ReLU()
    ...
    (Organization): ReLU()
  ) 
  (jk): ModuleDict(
    (Concept): JumpingKnowledge(cat)
    (Person): JumpingKnowledge(cat)
    ...
    (Organization): JumpingKnowledge(cat)
  )
  (lin): ModuleDict(
    (Concept): Linear(in_features=16, out_features=16, bias=True)
    (Person): Linear(in_features=16, out_features=16, bias=True)
    (Organization): Linear(in_features=16, out_features=16, bias=True)
  )
)
```
:::

## Learned Embeddings: Concepts

![](./figures/concept_embeddings.png){width=70% fig-align="center"}

PCA visualization reveals meaningful clustering: classical ML techniques separate from deep learning methods, with general concepts occupying intermediate regions.

## Learned Embeddings: Researchers

![](./figures/people_embedding.png){width=70% fig-align="center"}

Neural network researchers (Hinton, Hopfield, Hebb) cluster together, while AI pioneers distribute across the space reflecting broader domain influence.

# Conclusions

## Where and How to Use It?

**Applications:**

1. 💡 **Company knowledge extraction** and structuring;
2. 🔍 **Systematic analysis of sources** (e.g., literature reviews, overviews);
3. 🏷️ **Entity extraction and similarity search** based on structural embeddings;
4. ❓ **Domain-specific question answering** with structural grounding;
5. 🧺 **Enhanced recommendation systems** leveraging graph structure.


::: {.callout-tip}
# Recommended flow

 **STAGE 1** Text ➡️ LLM extraction ➡️ Knowledge Graph (text-based)
 
 **STAGE 2** Knowledge Graph ➡️ Hetero Graph (numerical) ➡️ GNN training ➡️ Downstream tasks
:::


## Key Findings

::: {.larger}

💡 **Methods and techniques**

- 📄 **Text-to-knowledge-graph transformation** is feasible using LLMs;
- 🧠 **GNN training** on LLM-extracted graphs enables link prediction after numerical encoding;
- 🗺️ **Structure-aware embeddings** capture semantic relationships missed by text-only approaches.

🎓 **Research Directions**

- 🤖 **Foundation models** for:
  - Knowledge graph reasoning (Galkin et al., 2023)
  - Domain-specific graph classification (Zhao et al., 2024)
- 🛡️ **Reduced hallucination** through structural constraints (Zhao et al., 2024)
- ✨ **Enhanced reasoning** with structured data support (Zhou et al., 2025)

:::

---

## References {.smaller}

- Galkin, M., et al. (2023). Towards foundation models for knowledge graph reasoning. arXiv:2310.04562
- Gillani, K., et al. (2024). Knowledge graph extraction from textual data using LLM. Proceedings of SiKDD 2024
- Hamilton, W. L. (2020). Graph representation learning. Synthesis Lectures on AI and ML, 14(3), 1-159
- Huang, X., et al. (2025). How expressive are knowledge graph foundation models? arXiv:2502.13339
- Wójcik, F., & Malczewski, M. (2025). HeXtractor: Extracting heterogeneous graphs for GNNs. JOSS, 10(110), 8057
- Zhao, J., et al. (2024). GraphAny: A foundation model for node classification. arXiv:2405.20445
- Zhou, Z., et al. (2025). Landscape of thoughts: Visualizing LLM reasoning. arXiv:2503.22165

# Thank You!
